message: Blocks a change that brings in an outside package with a known security problem or a license problem. When a pull request adds a dependency, this checks the newly added packages against GitHub's record of known security problems and against their software licenses, and blocks the merge — naming the package and the problem in plain language, with a next step — if a package is known to be vulnerable, if its license can't be identified (so you may not even have the right to use it), or if it carries a strong 'copyleft' license that can require you to publish your own source code. That last one applies only on a private project, where your code is meant to stay closed; on a public project, where your source is already open, it isn't enforced. It only reads; it never changes a file. It looks at your project's own dependencies, not the engine's internal tooling. You can formally accept a specific advisory or license so the check passes — a deliberate, acknowledged change your engine can make for you, never a silent one. The data is free on public projects and, on private projects, comes with GitHub's paid code-security feature; where that data isn't available the check says so plainly and explains the cost and benefit, so it never passes silently and never blocks you for a reason it can't show.
